data.title: Opera: Our Buy Zone Is Here - Rich Returns Ahead
data.description:
OPRA Is Finally Near Our Buy Zone After The Much Needed Selloff

We previously covered Opera Limited (NASDAQ:OPRA) in October 2024, discussing its robust prospects attributed to the improved monetization in Western regions, the recently refreshed Opera One for the iOS browser, and the 14 consecutive quarters of Rule of 40 financial outperformance across revenue/ EBITDA growth.

Combined with the compelling valuations, the rich forward dividend yields, and the double digits upside potential, we had reiterated our Buy rating upon a moderate retracement to our recommended Buy zone at between $14s and $16s for an improved margin of safety.

OPRA 1Y Stock Price

Since then, OPRA has further rallied by +20.2% to chart new 52 week heights of $22.50, before rapidly selling off by mid February 2025, attributed to the bursting AI bubble, the mixed macroeconomic/ geopolitical environment, and the higher recessionary risks.

Even so, we believe that the meltdown has been overly done, given that the global advertising spending is still expected to grow by +10.7% YoY to $1.08T in 2025, with digital ad spending expected to comprise over 75% of overall "media ad spending for the first time."

This is a relevant information indeed, since advertising is increasingly becoming OPRA's top-line driver at $93.32M (+21.4% QoQ/ +37.5% YoY) while comprising 63.9% of its FQ4'24 revenues (+1.6 points QoQ/ +3.9 YoY) - a promising development, since advertising is a typical highly profitable end market.

This is on top of its expanding partnership with Alphabet (GOOG), amongst others, with it contributing to the growing search revenues of $52.32M (+13% QoQ/ +17% YoY).

It is apparent that OPRA's intensified marketing/ AI-related/ data center investments have paid off extremely well, with it already triggering the robust top-line growth, as discussed above.

The same has been observed in the growing users from western markets by +53% and ARPU growth by 3.9x over the past four years to $1.97 in FQ4'24 (+18.6% QoQ/ +36.8% YoY), with it underscoring the management's ability to increasingly monetize its offerings, significantly aided by the higher Opera GX gaming browser users at 33.9M (+6.2% QoQ/ +21.9% YoY).

These reasons are also why we are not overly concerned about OPRA's moderating adj EBITDA margins to 22.6% in FQ4'24 (-2.3 points QoQ/ -1.9 YoY/ +9 from FY2019 levels of 13.6%) and adj EPS to $0.28 (-45% QoQ/ -28.2% YoY).

This is especially given the richer balance sheet at a net cash position of $126.79M (+35% YoY), allowing the management to incrementally invest in its long-term growth opportunities, as the North America and the EU region increasingly comprise 19% of its user base (+3 points YoY).

The Consensus Forward Estimates

This is especially since OPRA has already offered a promising FY2025 revenue guidance of $572M at the midpoint (+19% YoY) with stable adj EBITDA margins of 23.6% (-0.3 points YoY), with it building upon the robust FY2024 growth at +21.1% YoY and 23.9% (+0.3 points YoY), respectively.

These reasons may also be why the consensus have raised their forward estimates, with the browser company expected to report an accelerated top/ bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +16.4%/ +19.7% through FY2027.

This is compared to the original estimates of +10.7%/ +16.5%, while accelerating from the historical 5Y top-line growth at a CAGR of +7.5%.

Thanks to the richer net cash flow from operating activities at $104.97M (+26.8% YoY), it is apparent that OPRA remains well capitalized to pursue accretive investments and shareholder returns as well, as observed in the higher FY2024 capex by +1,148% YoY and ongoing share retirement by -24.3% since FY2020.

OPRA Valuations

These reasons are also why OPRA continues to be highly compelling at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 14.11x, compared to the 1Y mean of 15.47x and the 5Y mean of 23.75x.

The same may be observed in its cheap FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 0.78x, compared to the sector median of 1.56x and its diversified browser peers, including Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) at 2.37x and Alphabet Inc. (GOOG), (GOOGL) at 1.07x, underscoring why OPRA continues to offer a promising high growth investment thesis, made more attractive by the recent correction.

So, Is OPRA Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

OPRA 5Y Stock Price

For now, OPRA has lost all of its Q4'24 and Q1'25 gains, with the stock breaching numerous support levels while trading below its 50/ 100/ 200 day moving averages.

Even so, we believe that the correction has been a boon indeed, with the stock already entering our Buy zones while nearing the updated fair value estimates of $14.80, based on the FY2024 adj EPS of $0.96 and the 1Y P/E mean of 15.47x.

It has also triggered a richer upside potential of +57.9% to our long-term price target of $25.50, based on the consensus raised FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $1.65.

If anything, building upon the share retirement discussed above, OPRA also pays out rich annualized dividends of $0.80 per share with forward dividend yields of 4.95% at the time of writing, compared to the 4Y averages of 3.73% and the sector median of 1.68%.

Thanks to the robust dual pronged return prospects through capital appreciation and dividend incomes, we are reiterating our Buy rating, with the caveat the investors observe the ongoing correction for a little longer.

Given the higher CBOE Volatility Index and the still elevated short interest ratio at 9.7%, it remains to be seen if OPRA's recent bounce from $15 ranges may last, with market volatility likely to persist for a little longer until sentiments surrounding the mixed macroeconomic/ geopolitical environment lift.

Patience may be more prudent in the mean time.

data.title: ECB's March Meeting Minutes Confirm Diverging Views On The Timing Of Further Cuts
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski

The most important passage of the European Central Bank's latest minutes was the discussion on the monetary policy outlook. It is obvious that at the March meeting, the views on next steps had already started to diverge. The more dovish ECB members stressed the risk of inflation undershooting as a result of escalating trade tensions, but also a stronger euro. On the other hand, the more hawkish team focused on sticky services and domestic inflation as well as the fiscal policy U-turn in Germany, justifying a more cautious approach to further rate cuts. As a result, few members argued that they “could only support the proposal to reduce interest rates by a further 25 basis points if there was also a change in communication that avoided any indication of future cuts or of the future direction of travel."

Looking ahead, the crucial question will be whether last night’s tariff announcements will convince the more hawkish team to agree to further rate cuts. The dovish team definitely seems to be on board. In short, if US tariffs on European goods are indeed implemented on April 9, this darkens the near-term outlook for the eurozone economy. We see little upside risk to inflation, unless the EU reacts with harsh retaliatory measures - something we don’t expect. Therefore, we think that last night’s negative trade and confidence shock and little prospect of quick negotiations will bring enough hawks into the team to cut the policy rate again by 25bp at the ECB’s April meeting.

Market Review

  • Markets exhibited broader positive performance over the third quarter. Key performance drivers included the first of several expected rate cuts in 2024, where the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) implemented a 50 basis point (bp) cut in policy rates. There were also increased expectations for further rate cuts in 2024, driven by a more dovish shift from the Fed and hopes for an economic soft landing. This shift was influenced by mixed economic data, including softer consumer price index (CPI) reports and fluctuating job market indicators, which sparked recession fears but were mostly balanced by strong consumer spending and retail sales. Looking ahead, the bull case relies on continued Fed easing, disinflation and a stable labor market. However, high valuations, potential labor market issues, and wavering consumer confidence pose risks to this optimistic outlook.[1]
  • U.S. high yield bonds performed well over the quarter amid this environment, gaining +5.32%.[2] Short duration high yield bonds also performed well, returning +3.94%[3], yet underperformed broader high yield bonds due to its more limited duration exposure. The asset class benefitted from a contraction in high yield spreads, which moved 18 bps tighter, and a drop of around 100 bps in yield. Within high yield, lower-quality credit led the way with CCCs up +10.40%, outperforming both Bs and BBs which returned +4.59% and +4.27%, respectively.[4] Cable and Satellite TV, Telecommunications and Broadcasting were the top performing high yield sectors. In contrast, Automotive, Food & Drug Retail and Railroad sectors lagged. High yield issuance also continued to be robust with approximately $74 billion in new supply during the third quarter, following $78 billion in the second quarter and $87 billion in the first. 5 Default volume also remained relatively benign. The par-weighted U.S. high yield bond default rate finished the quarter around 1.64% over the last twelve months, which is more than 100 bps lower than it was at the start of the year.[5]

Fund Review

  • The Fund returned +4.18%, reflecting performance at the net asset value (NAV) of Class I shares with all distributions reinvested for the quarter ended September 30, 2024. The Fund’s benchmark, the ICE BofA HY US Corp Cash Pay BB-B 1-5Yrs USD Index*, returned +3.94% during the same period.
  • Security selection within the Telecommunications sector contributed to relative performance. The sector was one of the stronger performing segments of the high yield index over the quarter, and the Fund was overweight several lower-rated issuers that benefited from a lower interest rate environment and increased corporate activity around merger & acquisitions. Credit selection within Healthcare sector also contributed, specifically from underweight positions to the Pharmaceutical and Health Services subsectors, as these positions underperformed the index. Security selection within the Transportation sector also contributed, reflected by overweights to positions in the Air Freight and Logistics subsector that outperformed. An allocation to CCC credit also contributed to relative performance over the quarter, with lower-rated credit tiers outperforming higher-quality bonds over the period.
  • The Fund’s overweight allocation to and security selection within the Energy sector was a detractor from relative performance. The sector underperformed amid slowing growth prospects and a meaningful decline in oil prices. The Fund specifically held overweight positions in select E&P issuers, which were impacted most by these headwinds. Selection within the Basic Industry sector also detracted, reflected by the Fund’s overweights to positions in the Metals and Mining and Chemicals subsectors that also underperformed amid softer commodity prices. Security selection within the Automotive sector detracted from relative performance as the Fund was underweight companies in the Automakers subsector that outperformed due to idiosyncratic reasons.

Fund Positioning

  • We continued to be overweight B-rated credits.

The Fund remained overweight allocation to Bs relative to the benchmark, while underweight BBs. We believe that exposure to lower-rated credit currently offers the best relative value as we remain constructive on credit in general given the increased prospects for a soft-landing scenario. However, we increased the Fund’s exposure to BBs towards the latter end of the quarter as a reflection of concerns around economic growth. The Fund also maintained a modest allocation to CCCs, as we look to be selective in sourcing these potential investments that present more idiosyncratic opportunities as opposed to adding broader risk exposure.

  • We are more balanced in the Fund’s sector exposure.

The Fund remained overweight the Basic Industry and Energy sectors, which continued to be the top overweight sectors by a meaningful margin. However, we reduced this overweight, specifically in Chemicals, Metals and Mining, and E&P subsectors, as these areas tend to be sensitive to lower commodity prices and slower economic growth. Instead, we added to more defensive sectors, such as Utilities and Healthcare. The Fund’s top sector underweights remained Media and Services, albeit at more modest levels relative to prior quarters.

  • We added to sectors that have historically benefited from a lower interest rate environment.

With the Fed expected to implement further rate cuts in the near term, we have shifted focus to adding exposure in sectors that should benefit from a lower interest rate environment. These include sectors like Financial Services and Real Estate, as well as select opportunities in Telecommunications where certain companies have a more positive outlook amid a relatively more favorable refinancing environment.

  • The Fund maintained modest exposure to off-benchmark sectors.

We generally believe that select exposures to non-high yield sectors can offer attractive risk-reward opportunities, potential portfolio diversification benefits, and avenues for liquidity. While the Fund has the flexibility to toggle allocations to off-benchmark asset classes, such as bank loans and convertible bonds, we continue to view high yield bonds as relatively attractive compared to other sectors.

Outlook

  • We remain constructive on high yield credit despite the likely outcome of moderating economic growth.

We look to be more dynamic going forward given the potential for market fluctuations in response to above trend growth slowing to a pace more akin to trend. The Fund currently holds a more balanced composition between cyclical and defensive sectors, as we have reduced areas like Energy and added to rate beneficiaries like Real Estate and Utilities. Notably, fundamentals for high yield issuers continue to be robust with leverage and interest rate coverage also remain at supportive levels, aiding the belief that defaults should be relatively benign as we move into 2025. Key risk factors to watch remain a resurgence of inflation that upends the narrative accommodative monetary policy, a pivot by issuers to more non-credit- friendly uses of debt finance, and a macro or policy driven spike of interest rate volatility that would leave investors flipping to defensive with less focus on credit.

data.title: lululemon athletica: President Trump's Reciprocal Tariffs Kill The Turnaround Story For Now
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The last time I wrote about Lululemon Athletica (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:LULU), back in December 2024, I discussed the company’s third quarter performance and analyzed the company’s progress made towards stabilizing the declines seen in the Americas as well as whether the company’s momentum in its international markets remained sustainable. I had a BUY rating on the company.

Since the article was published, the company’s stock has tanked nearly 30%, including a more than 15% drop following its Q4 report, significantly underperforming the S&P 500, which declined 7% during the same period.

In this article, I analyze the company’s Q4 earnings report, which was released last week, and investigate how the company plans to navigate what is expected to be a challenging macroenvironment, especially in the company’s biggest market, North America.

A Snapshot of LULU’s Q4 Performance

LULU had a strong finish to the year in my opinion. More specifically, Q4 revenues came in at $3.61 billion, up 12.68% y/y and beating analyst estimates by $30.3 million. Diluted EPS came in at $6.14, up 16.1% y/y and comfortably beating analyst estimates by $0.27.

Gross margins came in very strong at 60.4%, which translates to an expansion of 100 bps (1%). Operating margins also were impressive, jumping 40 bps (0.4%) to 28.9%.

Despite a strong quarter, the stock crashed 14.2% post the earnings release and has been on a free fall since then. This drop can be attributed to a softer-than-expected guidance, both for FY25 and for the first quarter of FY25, as the company also fell victim to the punishing tariff policies as well as the overly cautious US consumer.

More specifically, for FY25, the company now expects net revenues come in between $11.15 billion and $11.3 billion (5% to 7% y/y growth), which fell short of the consensus estimates of $11.31 billion. Diluted EPS is now expected to be between $14.95 and $15.15, which fell considerably short of the $15.40 that analysts were expecting. For the first quarter of FY25, the company expects net revenue to be in the range between $2.335 billion and $2.355 billion, which fell short of analysts' expectations of $2.40 billion. Diluted EPS is expected to be between $2.53 and $2.58 versus analysts’ estimates of $2.74. Investors have to keep in mind, however, that the company still has $1.3 billion allocated solely to undertake future share repurchases.

Reciprocal Tariffs Kill LULU's Turnaround Story For Now

LULU cannot catch a break in my opinion. The company began last year with a major misstep as it failed to capitalize on the strong demand seen in the women’s category due to a failure to adequately stock up those choices that were in demand. At the same time, it was facing increasing competition from the likes of Alo and Vuori, who were also eating into the company’s market share. These twin shocks led to a substantial decline in growth in the U.S. market, and it took the company quite a while to stabilize.

By the third quarter, the Americas had returned to growth once again, and the decline in sales had stopped in the U.S. It also had a stellar holiday season, which enabled it to register strong margins as it did not have to rely on any markdowns. The quarter gone by was no different, as both revenues and EPS saw strong growth, and the gross margins once again expanded, further demonstrating the ability of the company to manage its sales without relying on markdowns. Net revenues in both Americas and in its International markets were impressive, and while the comparable sales in the Americas were flat, international comparable sales, largely due to China, grew 22% y/y on a constant dollar basis.

The company’s product launches, which included the likes of Glow Up, Daydrift and BeCalm, have been a hit with consumers, further demonstrating that the company’s ability to innovate continues to be strong and that the misstep of last year was a one-off shock. Daydrift, which is the company’s latest “lifestyle trouser for women,” has seen strong sales to the extent that the company now expects it to be a new core franchise going forward. The company also announced a strong product pipeline for 2025.

Finally, the company’s international markets continue to maintain its growth momentum. China, in particular, has been particularly impressive, as evidenced by the 39% y/y growth in revenues and a 27% y/y growth in comparable sales. This is an improvement on the previous quarter, when revenue growth was at 36% and comparable sales were at 23%. While the growth in both these metrics have not reached the levels seen in the first quarter, it has been heading in the positive direction with each passing quarter. Investors should also note that the steady increase in growth in China is happening despite the unaided brand awareness being at mid-to-high-teens.

Finally, the company continues to rely on its unique marketing strategy of community-based brand activations as well as launching member-only events to boost its membership program, which now stands at 28 million, an approximately 40% increase since the start of the year. Q4 was no different, as evidenced by events such as Membership Madness, where members gained exclusive access to in-store events and free classes at studio partners, opening a Glow Up Studio in connection with the launch of Glow Up, and partnership with Rock ‘n’ Roll Half Marathon in Las Vegas. All of these unique events paid off, as evidenced by the strong top- and bottom-line growth.

Overall, the company has taken all the right steps in my opinion, to correct the missteps of the first quarter of last year, and ensure that the company was back on the right track, especially amidst rising competition. Unfortunately, for the company, just as it was steadying the ship, the macroeconomic environment has become a lot more challenging, especially in the U.S., courtesy of tariffs and a US consumer who is devoid of confidence and has become overly cautious.

Every economic data related to the U.S. consumer has been flashing red recently. For instance, the University of Michigan’s consumer sentiment index, in mid-March came in at 57.9 from 64.7 in February, which translates to a y/y decline of 27%. The figure was the lowest level since November 2022. Spending on luxury items fell 9.3% y/y in February, much worse than the 5.9% y/y decline seen in January, which demonstrates that even high-income consumers, who make up a significant portion of LULU’s customers are cutting down on their spending as well. Then, there was the Conference Board’s consumer confidence index, which fell to 92.9 in March, marking its fourth consecutive month of decline. At the same time, the forward-looking expectations component of the index, came in at 65.2, which is a 12-year low. Finally, while the CPI report came in better-than-expected in March, the Fed’s preferred inflation gauge, the PCE index came in hotter-than-expected, further stoking fears of inflation picking up. Finally, from a U.S. economy perspective, in the latest Chicago Fed National Activity Index (CFNAI), the employment related indicators contributed +0.02, which was down from +0.08. While the sales, orders, and inventories indicators rose marginally, they still came in at -0.01. It was a similar picture for personal consumption and housing, which while jumping from -0.14 to -0.01, still showed a decline.

All the aforementioned economic data suggests that the US consumer has become cautious and has a negative outlook on the future of the economy. At the same time, if one looks at the guidance provided by WMT, a bellwether of the US economy, it also shows how the US consumer has significantly cut down on discretionary spending, an issue that was flagged by LULU’s management as well. Furthermore, investors, economists, and the US consumer in general, still have no clue the full extent of the reciprocal tariffs and what impact it will have, both in the short-term and medium-term on the US economy. For now, LULU’s management has projected FY25 gross margins to decline 60 bps, of which, approximately 20 bps is attributed to tariffs. However, the 20-bps headwind doesn’t take into account any impact from the impending reciprocal tariffs, which suggests that there is a possibility that it could get worse than expected. According to the company 2023 Annual report, 42% of its products were manufactured in Vietnam, and 10% were manufactured in Indonesia. 40% of the company’s fabrics were sourced from Taiwan. All these nations are in the so-called ‘Dirty 15’ countries and they were hit with massive reciprocal tariffs. More specifically, the tariff rates for Vietnam was 46%, Taiwan was 32%, and Indonesia was slapped with 32%. Additionally, China, one of the biggest international markets for LULU, was slapped with an additional 34%.

Overall, LULU has done a wonderful job in my opinion to steady its ship after a bizarre misstep in the first quarter led to a sales decline. Since then, the company’s ability to innovate and the various marketing strategies to boost awareness, have all paid off, as evidenced by the stabilizing sales in the U.S. At the same time, the international markets, driven by China, continues to perform remarkably well, with growth in revenues and comparable sales increasing sequentially. The latest products have all been a hit and the company’s 2025 product pipeline has been built keeping in mind the demand from its consumers. From a long-term perspective, the “innovation and newness” in the company’s products should continue to drive growth. For the foreseeable future, however, it is going to be choppy waters for the company courtesy of a more challenging macroenvironment, which just got a whole lot worse with President Trump’s reciprocal tariffs. Unlike the first quarter, the company cannot be blamed for today’s challenges, but unfortunately, the external challenges are far too powerful.

Valuation

Forward P/E Approach

Price Target

$341.00

Projected Forward P/E Multiple

22.8x

Projected FY25 EPS

$14.95

Source: Company’s Q4 Earnings Release, LSEG Data (formerly Refinitiv), and Author’s Calculations

As mentioned earlier, both the revenue and EPS guidance for FY25 fell well short of analysts’ expectations, which was the reason behind the stock plummeting in excess of 15% post the earnings release. More specifically, the company now expects FY25 EPS to come in between $14.95 and $15.15, versus analysts’ expectations of $15.40.

If one looks at LULU’s results historically, then the company has had a track record of beating both analysts’ expectations as well as the high-end of its own guidance on a consistent basis.

As evidenced by the table above, on average, the company has beaten the high-end of its guidance by 10.13%. While this is impressive, one has to take into account that the FY21 beat has heavily skewed the results. If, however, we look at the difference between actual EPS and analyst EPS, then it has somewhat stayed consistent over the years, and the average magnitude of the beat is 1.81%. If I assumed that LULU would beat analyst estimates by 1.81% in FY25, it would have resulted in a projected FY25 EPS of $15.68. This is an assumption that I would have made, had President Trump’s reciprocal tariffs not been harsher-than-expected.

Unfortunately for LULU, they did, and given that the management, in their guidance only considered the impact from the initial tariffs on Mexico and Canada into their guidance, I expect the impact on the company’s bottom line to be far worse. As such, conservatively, I have assumed the lower end of the company’s guidance for my calculations, which are $14.95.

A projected FY25 EPS of $14.95 would imply an EPS growth of 2.2% y/y, which is a reasonable estimate, especially since the reciprocal tariffs were slapped on countries where the company sources a significant portion of its materials from and given the challenging macroenvironment driven by tariffs and a cautious US consumer. The 5-year historical median P/E of the company has been 31.7x. The projected EPS growth is about 28% of the long-term average EPS growth of 7.9%. As such, I have assumed a forward P/E that is at a 28% discount to the 5-year historical median P/E, which would be 22.8x, significantly lower than my previous estimate of 28.2x.

At a forward P/E of 22.8 and a projected EPS of $14.95, my updated price target for LULU is $341, significantly lower than my previous PT of $451, but still represents an upside of about 35% from current levels, especially after accounting for the additional 11% drop at the time of writing. Investors should also note that the short-term momentum in the stock is broken, as evidenced by the D grade on the Seeking Alpha Momentum Scale.

Overall, despite the considerable upside from current levels, I am still maintaining my BUY rating on the stock, rather than upgrading it to STRONG BUY. Moreover, I would strongly recommend a dollar-cost averaging strategy. This is also because there are considerable headwinds for the stock in the near term, which would imply much higher volatility. I have a very small position in LULU, but at considerably higher price levels than where it is trading now. Despite this, I am still waiting for the noise surrounding the reciprocal tariffs to quieten down before adding more to my existing position.

Risk Factors

My risk factors remain the same as my last article. The situation in North America in general, and the U.S., continues to remain challenging. As mentioned earlier, the management, during the earnings call, reiterated that they “remain focused on what we can control.” My bull case predominantly rests on the premise that the “newness and innovation,” should eventually make up, in the second half of the year, for the slowdown in sales growth in the first half of the year, after consumers get adjusted to the new tariff-induced normal. Any further delays to pick up in consumer spending can be detrimental to both revenue and EPS growth, especially as the company continues to make investments in line with its Power of Three x 2 strategy.

In addition to increasing competition in the U.S., there is also the possibility that a full-blown global trade war could have a negative impact on sales of the company’s international markets, especially China, where the company has been registering impressive growth. The odds of this have considerably increased now that the Trump Administration have imposed harsher tariffs than expected. Should this permanently materialize, then it would be an additional shock that the company would have to deal with, which would once again dampen the company’s growth.

Concluding Thoughts

LULU ended the year on a high, with a strong beat on both the top- and bottom-line estimates. The company’s sales in the US stabilized whereas it’s international markets continued their positive momentum. After a bad start to the year due to the company’s own errors, management has steadied the ship. Unfortunately for investors, the ship is heading into choppy waters, in the form of a challenging macroenvironment driven by a cautious US consumer and increased odds of a full-blown trade war thanks to harsher-than-expected reciprocal tariffs imposed by the Trump Administration. Even the management’s guidance for FY25 did not account for the latter.

From a valuation perspective, the stock following the post-earnings selloffs as well as the post-reciprocal tariff selloff continues to be attractive at current levels. However, its momentum has completely broken down. As such, while I am maintaining my BUY rating on the stock, just like the sentiment of the US consumer today, caution is warranted for both existing and potential investors.

data.title: Blue-Chip Dividends Entering Bargain Territory: 2 Names To Buy
data.description:
Since the new President took office, the economy has become more uncertain. On the one hand, there is a justified assumption of experiencing a decent boost to the economy through an easier political decision-making and the promise to stimulate local supply chains. But on the other hand, we see such things as the introduction of tariffs and increasing geopolitical tensions that have completely opposite effects on the economy.

This kind of uncertainty has muted the growth expectations that the market had priced in across various indices. The Nasdaq-100 (QQQ) is the best example to take in order to see these effects. Namely, the index is down ~8% on a YTD basis and the underlying volatility or the VIX has become rather elevated.

In fact, it is not only pure-play growth companies that have experienced a decline in their valuations, but also some value-oriented names as well. The S&P 500 (SPY), which has a decent concentration of value stocks, is down ~4% on a YTD basis.

In other words, the recent market dynamics have created some interesting opportunities for long-term investors to consider.

In my view, we currently also have the right environment for high quality and income-based investing. Since the overall growth prospects seem quite challenging and the odds for a recession have increased, the equity story for defensive value plays has improved accordingly.

If we can get both things - high quality factor and relatively low/attractive valuations - then I think we should at least dedicate some time to consider such opportunities.

In the article below, I will elaborate on two picks, which embody both of these factors, including an enticing yield angle, which is critical for me as a prudent and long-term income investor.

#1 - Alexandria Real Estate Equities

Alexandria Real Estate Equities, Inc. (ARE) is a high-quality equity REIT that has been completely misunderstood by the market.

Theoretically, we could find grounds for having ARE discounted:

  • Unfavorable supply/demand balance in the office space (driven by the work-from-home dynamic).
  • Higher-for-longer imposing damaging refinancing risk.
  • AI decreasing the need for a lab space.

As a result of such a consensus in the market, ARE is trading at a dirt cheap multiple. Here is a visual representation of the P/CF dynamic.

Over the past three years, ARE's P/CF multiple has declined by almost 70%. We have to also keep in mind that the actual valuation level has decreased even more than this because during the 3-year period ARE has managed to register a growing FFO per share figure.

The question is whether all of this is justified.

In my humble opinion, the answer is a clear no.

First, none of the three aforementioned (negative) aspects have a meaningful impact on ARE and, second, ARE's fundamentals remain just as strong as in the period before the share price started to plunge.

Here are the key facts that prove that.

First, ARE has an upper investment grade credit rating, which is a very rare thing in the equity REIT space. It allows ARE to source in cheap debt and have the necessary flexibility on the maturity structure end. For example, currently, ARE has only 14% of its total debt expiring over the next three-year period. Hence, the negative refinancing risk here is very immaterial.

Second, ARE's leasing performance clearly indicates that there is a strong and healthy demand for its properties. Even though the leasing volume in 2024 was 17% above that of the previous year, the REIT still managed to secure leases at attractive spreads, while maintaining an excellent occupancy (i.e., 94.6%). For example, the releasing cash basis spread of 7.2% with a ~9.5 years of average lease maturity term does not give the impression that ARE's properties have gone out of vogue.

Third, and this is somewhat related to the previous point, ARE is a strongly growing business; and the financials prove that. In 2024, the business generated FFO per share growth of 5.6% and the Q4 FFO per share figure advanced by 6% on a year-to-year basis.

While the market has priced in a slight FFO decline in 2025 (due to temporary vacancies, non-core asset divestitures, and organic CapEx projects that start to come online in 2026), the future trajectory of the FFO performance looks solid.

In a nutshell, the P/FFO of 9.8x seems way too low relative to what we get by buying ARE.

The business is growing, backed by a robust capital structure and offers an enticing yield of 5.7%, which is underpinned by one of the most conservative FFO payout ratios in the sector (i.e., ~55%).

#2 - Brookfield Infrastructure Partners

Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP)(BIP.UN:CA)(BIPC) has a similar story to ARE. It is a robust business with steadily growing cash flows that has been subject to unfavorable repricing by the market. Over the past three years, BIP's share (or unit) price has dropped by ~33%. During this exact time period, the FFO per share has consistently increased without suffering any hiccups in the process.

Currently, BIP's P/FFO stands at 9.1x and the distribution yield is 5.6%.

The theoretical reason for the decline in BIP's valuation is tied to the combination of higher-for-longer interest rate scenario and inflation uncertainties. The higher the interest rates, the more challenging it is for BIP to achieve positive final-investment-decisions (i.e., it makes new infrastructure projects more expensive, lowering the demand from the buyers' side). Plus, there is the same argument of unfavorable refinancings just as in ARE's case. Since BIP is inherently a heavy-leverage business, these dynamics have to be taken seriously.

However, if we look deeper into BIP, we will arrive at the same conclusion as above for ARE: i.e., the market has got this wrong.

First, BIP has a strong investment grade credit rating and 90% of the borrowings are fixed, which in combination with ~8-year weighted average maturity is a significant mitigant for the refinancing risk. Furthermore, the lion's share of BIP's debt is assumed on a non-recourse basis, which means that in case any specific project goes belly up, BIP can just as easily hand the keys back to the lenders without having to cover the losses through its own balance sheet. It has not used it before in any material fashion, but still, it is worth factoring in this nuance.

Second, BIP's FFO payout is 67%, which helps drive sustainable growth. In other words, through notable FFO retention, BIP can assume incremental CapEx spend in a more sustainable (not as debt dependent) fashion. Here, we have to also appreciate the fact that BIP can access additional capital from completed project sales. For instance, last year, the business accessed $2 billion by selling high IRR project stakes. This year, management plans to utilize this source more aggressively (which makes sense given that BIP had many completed projects in 2024), thereby further relaxing the pressure on the balance sheet.

Third, and this is important, I think it would be logical to assume a higher FFO growth rate going forward than in the past. Since 2020, the average FFO per share growth has been ~8%, which is already attractive enough in the context of a single digit P/FFO multiple.

So, the reason why I think that BIP could in the medium-term hit a low double-digit FFO per unit growth mark is nicely captured in the chart below:

As we can see, there is a significant chunk of CapEx that is earmarked towards much higher growth areas than the typical/traditional infrastructure, such as utilities and transport. The data front is what can generate high double-digit growth, thus pushing the overall portfolio results higher.

To illustrate this point, we can take a look at the data segment's performance in 2024. In 2024, BIP registered an FFO growth of 21% in the data segment. All of these cash flows stem from corporate offtakers (parties) that have long-term and CPI-linked contracts with BIP.

The bottom line

Oftentimes, when the market becomes a more volatile place, there are some attractive opportunities that emerge. In fact, these negative moves in the market can make already unfairly priced securities even more discounted.

This is exactly what has happened both for ARE and BIP.

As elaborated above in the article, in my view, both businesses are misunderstood by the market and being priced as low-quality securities despite having robust capital structures and defensive (and growing) cash flows.

data.title: Waste Management: Fast Grower, But Beware Of High Debt And Overvaluation
data.description:
Waste Management (NYSE:WM) is a simple company with a simple business model, that has seen great growth over the past few years. Their stock price has skyrocketed by 164.38% just over the past year, and so far, it doesn't show a sign of stopping. Sadly, based on my analysis, I do have to rate the company as a HOLD rather than a BUY, mainly due to their current overvaluation and high debt. In this short article, we will take a look at their latest Q4'24 earnings report and analyze their fundamentals through our 9 Pillar Analysis to finally calculate their intrinsic value.

Analysis

Q4'24 Earnings

First things first, let's start by looking at WM's short-term achievements. In Q4, the company actually reported a miss on their Non-GAAP EPS, reporting it at $1.70, $0.10 lower than the estimates of $1.80.

Their revenue, although increasing by 12.80% YOY, was also reported as a small miss by $20.00M, expected at $5.89B but reported at $5.87B. Regarding the net income of the company, that has increased by 21.29% YOY, from $493.00M to $598.00M.

The company is also innovating, reporting that the money saved from a 1000 expected job cuts will go towards renewable energy plants, as well as be returned to investors through FCF, and spent on artificial intelligence through the next years.

All of this news is great; financial growth shows no sign of stopping, and with innovation, it could be expected that revenue will start increasing at an even higher rate. But for now, these are just speculations that aren't based on evidence.

9 Pillars

Therefore, as I am a long-term value investor, rather than basing my investments on short-term news and trends, I like to base them on long-term historical fundamentals. By going through the 9 pillars, we will find all the flaws WM has, as well as its strengths, so let's get to it.

Above, you can see that the company gets a rating of 6/9, with three pillars in the red. The 5Y PE and P/FCF, along with the debt levels. First, let's take a look at the company's valuation.

Valuation (Pillars 1 and 2)

The 5Y PE is sitting rather high, at 27.04 which in my eyes is expensive for a waste management company, though it is still bearable. The issue though is that currently, the PE is sitting even higher, at 34.33, increasing at a 5Y CAGR of 6.06%. This means that through the past years, investors have been paying more and more for the company, inflating its stock price. Below, you can see a graph depicting the 5Y PE growth.

Regarding the P/FCF, that is currently sitting at a whopping 52.19, which in my opinion screams overvaluation. Over the past 5Y, this ratio has been growing at a YOY rate of 21.28%, which translates to investors willing to pay 21.28% more for the company's yearly net income every single year.

Financial Growth

Now, of course, this growth in premium is to an extent justified because the revenue of the company has been growing at a 5Y CAGR of 7.88%, increasing from $15.22B in 2020, to $22.06B in 2024, and accounting for a total growth of 44.94%

The main deal though is the net income, which has grown by 83.56% over the past 5 years, growing at a CAGR of 13.53% from $1.50B in 2020, to $2.75B in 2024. In my eyes, this is some phenomenal growth; I just don't agree with the current cost of the financials.

Even though the net income and revenue have been growing really well, the FCF has been somewhat lagging behind, growing by only 21.90% over the past 5Y, from $1.77B in 2020, to $2.16B in 2024.

Overall, WM is showing off some great growth, but as I already said, I don't think it deserves such a high valuation.

Debt (Pillar 9)

A further issue I have with the company is their really high debt levels, especially with their slow-growing FCF. Currently, their LTL / 5Y FCF - Dividends ratio is sitting at 7.77, which is much higher than the "lower than 3" ratio I would like to see. Essentially, what this means is that with the company's 5Y average yearly FCF ($2.00B), it would take them about 36 years to pay off all their LTL ($30.06B) while paying out their dividends.

What I like even less is that WM hasn't been at all paying their LTL off; instead, they have been growing them at a CAGR of 11.27%, from $18.34B in 2020, to the current number of $30.06B. In my eyes, this puts the company in a vulnerable position if there were to come financial hardship. Moreover, with such high debt and P/FCF ratio, their value decreases drastically, which we will see in the DCF model, which we will go through at the end of the analysis.

Dividends (Pillar 8)

Talking about debt and FCF, let's take a look at the company's dividend sustainability. Their 5Y FCF payout ratio is sitting at a decent level of 52.48%, which means that after paying out their dividends, the company still has enough FCF to push into innovation and the previously mentioned paying down of debt.

With their dividends seeming quite safe, WM is looking like a great company for dividend growth investors, mainly thanks to their 5Y dividend CAGR of 8.35%.

Remaining Pillars

Before calculating the intrinsic value of the company, let's first look at their profitability and share buybacks. Something I like is that their profit margin has been increasing at a CAGR of 4.97%, from 9.83% in 2020 to 12.45% in 2024. Their ROIC has also been nice and stable, keeping at a 5Y average of 9.57%.

Regarding their share buybacks, they haven't been doing much of them in 2024, because, as we know, they have been trading quite overvalued. What I like though is that through 2022 to 2023, they have bought back around 3.82% of their shares outstanding, returning great value to investors.

Overall, I would say that the company is looking healthy, except for their high debt. Their overvaluation is a whole other story, though. It doesn't mean that when a business is good and growing, you can overpay for it. I would personally rather buy a cheaper company, and wait for a good, overvalued business to drop in price before potentially buying into it.

Intrinsic Value

Anyway, now that we have gone through the fundamentals, let's figure out the intrinsic value of the company, which, based on the PE and P/FCF, I believe will come out lower than the current price. We will kick off the valuation, as always, with the Graham's Formula.

As you can see above, to get the intrinsic value, I first plugged in the 5Y EPS of $5.39. After that, I predicted a 5Y EPS CAGR of 15.67%, which in my eyes is the most realistic EPS growth the company could experience in the future. After combining these two values and plugging them into the formula alongside the other values you see on the diagram, I got an intrinsic value of $170.16, 27.02% lower than the current trading price of $233.15.

The second valuation model we will look to is the DCF model, where I will plug in a 10Y + FCF CAGR at 6.30%, and combine it with a discount rate of 7.00% (my expected YOY return). After adding up all the discounted FCF, we have to subtract the debt, and that is where we run into a problem.

As I mentioned above, the excessive debt of the company will cause problems in its valuation. It currently makes up more than 33.33% of the company's discounted FFCF. Therefore, when we subtract it, accounting for the low cash and cash equivalents, the intrinsic value comes out to only $93.89. If there was no debt, the value would be $155.91.

For the third valuation model, we will compare WM to two of its competitors in the Multiples Valuation: Republic Services and Rollins. We will take these companies' PE ratios and average them out to a whopping 47.18. This shows that compared to its industry, WM might after all not be as overvalued as we thought, as when we multiply this PE with the current EPS of WM sitting at $6.81, we will get an intrinsic value of $321.30, quite higher than the current price

Finally, we will look to the alternative DDM, where I will predict the future dividend CAGR at 8.00% and get an intrinsic value of $256.49.

After averaging out the four intrinsic values, we will arrive to the ultimate intrinsic value sitting at $210.52, 9.83% lower than the current price of $233.47. With a margin of safety of 10.00% our acceptable buy price would be $189.47, which is the price at which I have the company on my watch list.

Conclusion

In conclusion, I have to rate Waste Management as a HOLD. This decision is based on their current overvaluation and high debt, though it is softened by the company's high financial growth and overall healthy financials with great dividend value. I believe that the company has a bright future ahead; I just trust that based on its current overvaluation, it might soon face a crush, which, if that were to happen, I would certainly return and re-rate the company, possibly giving them a BUY rating.

data.title: PVH Q4: Financial Improvements Aren't Big Enough (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
In complete contrast with my Buy rating on it, the owner of brands like Tommy Hilfiger and Calvin Klein, PVH Corp. (PVH), has dropped by 27% since the last time I covered the stock in August last year and has fallen slightly more in the past 12 months (see chart below).

But following its full year 2024 (year ending February 2025) results at the end of March, it jumped by nothing less than 18% in a single day. Could this finally be the start of a secular of rise in PVH? Let's find out.

Non-GAAP EPS makes notable gains...

The highlight of PVH's latest numbers is the earnings per share [EPS], specifically the non-GAAP EPS. This number was supported by gains due to restructuring and income tax gains among others, which reflect as follows:

  • At USD $11.74, it slightly exceeded expectations, with the guidance range of USD $11.55-11.70. Compared to the midpoint of the range, it was around 1% higher.
  • The final number is even more impressive when seen in the context of the fact that the guidance itself was upgraded through the year. The midpoint of the eventual guidance range was 6.9% higher than the initial range of USD 10.75-11. It follows then, that the actual number has taken a notable leap from there, ahead of the initial guidance by ~8%.
  • The non-GAAP EPS also grew by ~10% year-on-year (YoY) despite a contraction in revenues.
  • In Q4 2024 too, the non-GAAP EPS delivered a surprise. It came in 4.6% ahead of the midpoint of the range, though in this case it showed a 12% YoY contraction.

...but GAAP EPS is a mixed bag

The GAAP EPS doesn't paint as positive a picture, though it isn't bad either. At USD 10.56, the figure just about made it to the guidance range of USD 10.55-10.7. Similarly, the number for Q4 2024 was at USD 2.83, compared to the guidance range of USD 2.83-2.98.

Both the full year and Q4 2024 GAAP figures, however, were smaller than those seen last year. This is particularly so for the Q4 2024 number, which dropped by 38% YoY, even while the 2024 figure was down by a more acceptable 1.9%.

Operating margin and revenues as expected

With respect to the remainder of the results, the outcomes were as broadly as expected for both the operating margins and revenues. The details are as follows:

  • Non-GAAP operating margin on point.

The non-GAAP operating margin was almost on point, at 10%, compared to the guidance of 10.1%, but the GAAP margin was a bit lower at 8.9%, compared with the guidance of 9.2%. Even though profits could have benefited from the reduction in operating expenses, the decline in revenues turned out to be the overwhelming factor here.

  • Revenue declined as expected.

PVH Corp saw a 6% decline in revenue on a constant currency basis. This is at the lower end of the guidance range contraction of 6-7% or to put it another way, the decline was a shade lower than the midpoint of the range, which is some consolation. The revenue decline in the final quarter (Q4 2024), at 5% year-on-year (YoY), was actually smaller than the projected guidance range of 6-7%.

Despite the numbers being on target, though, it's hard to get past the fact that the company's sales are shrinking. This is troubling since only part of the decline is explained by the sale of the Heritage Brands' intimates segment. The company's big brands, Tommy Hilfiger and Calvin Klein, also saw a drop in revenues by 4.9% and 1.5% respectively.

Outlook and market multiples

The company's outlook for 2025 shows improvement, though. For one, it expects the revenue contraction to stop now, projecting them "to be flat to increase slightly." The non-GAAP operating margin is forecast to remain unchanged from 2024, while the non-GAAP EPS is expected to land in the range of USD 12.4-12.75. At the midpoint of the range, this reflects a 7.1% increase from 2024.

Assuming that PVH's EPS in fact comes in at the midpoint of the guidance range, the forward non-GAAP price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio comes in at just 6.2x. Not only is this lower than the 9.5x level it was at the last I checked, thanks to the price decline since, but it's also lower than the stock's five-year average of 10.6x. To put this in perspective, if the stock were to go back to trading at the five-year average, the price would have to rise by over 71%.

Can PVH make gains now?

The question now is whether PVH is in a position to make the kind of gains suggested by the forward P/E. Considering the risks, maybe not. But that doesn't mean it can't make gains at all.

A key risk right now is related to the consumer discretionary sector. Of all the major sectors, it's the only sector other than technology to see a weakening in performance year-to-date [YTD] (see chart below). This isn't for no reason either. Mild cooling off in the US economy was already expected for 2025 even last year, however, trade tariffs can have an additional impact on it. Specifically, they can put upward pressure on inflation, which has shown some resistance to softening in recent months in any case.

Next, the company hasn't been able to meet the big targets as per its restructuring plan called PVH+. Its revenues, for example, in 2025 are at best expected to grow just a tad, which would be around 30% lower than the target of USD $12.5 billion by the year as per the plan. Similarly, the company's operating margin target of 15% isn't expected to be achieved going by its latest outlook. These outcomes reduce confidence in the company.

At the same time, the fact that it was bang on about the financials for 2024 can't be ignored either. Neither can the fact that it upgraded its EPS guidance, and the actual numbers still surpassed it.

What next?

Owing to PVH's latest earnings numbers and expectation of improved revenue performance in 2025, there's still some reason to be positive on the stock. This is particularly important, as there's now a clear disconnect between the market multiples and the fundamentals. It might even make some gains this year on this account.

However, the equity markets and broader macro environment aren't favorably placed to support the consumer discretionary sector right now. These factors alone can negatively impact even stocks of high performing companies, so PVH stock is at even greater risk. I'm downgrading it to hold, on account of these developments. It would be good to wait and watch how the overall scenario evolves before buying it right now.

data.title: Lowe's May Face More Pain - Tariff Headwinds Not Priced In
data.description:
LOW May Face Further Macro Headwinds, With FY2025 Being A Painful Trough Year

We previously covered Lowe's Companies, Inc. (NYSE:LOW) in February 2024, discussing its robust performance metrics across growing profit margins, excellent shareholder returns, and lifting market sentiments as the Fed looked to pivot by H1'24.

Combined with the normalizing macroeconomy and recovery of resale housing market, we had believed that the stock offered a viable investment thesis then, significantly aided by the fair valuations and the management's intensified efforts to deliver improved one-stop-shop product offerings, expanded fulfilment options, and enhanced in-store/ digital experience.

LOW 1Y Stock Price

Since that Buy rating, LOW has charted an impressive upward rally by +20.1% to new 52 week heights of $287, before drastically pulling back by December 2024 as the Fed offers a hawkish rate cut, with the same pullback also observed in its peer and the wider market in varying degrees.

It is apparent by now that the inflation remains highly sticky, with the intensified tariff/ trade war and likely higher for longer interest rates already triggering recessionary risks, with the culmination of pessimistic developments contributing to the heightened CBOE Volatility Index and the market-wide selloff since mid-February 2025.

This is worsened by the still elevated 30Y Fixed Rate Mortgage Averages at 6.65% by March 27, 2025 and the 15Y rate at 5.89%, up from the September 19, 2024 bottom levels of 6.09%/ 5.15% and the 2019 averages of 4.13%/ 3.28%, respectively.

With borrowing costs still expensive and home prices still expensive, it is unsurprising why the market has turned pessimistic surrounding home improvement stocks, LOW included, attributed to the likely impacted DIY discretionary spending and Pro sales performance.

The same mixed demand has been observed in LOW's FQ4'24 earnings performance with comparable sales of +0.2% YoY, aided by the 100 basis points benefit from "storm-related demand from Hurricanes Helene and Milton" as "homeowners began the shift from securing their properties and clean-up towards recovery and rebuilding."

Otherwise, DIY discretionary spending remains impacted in FY2024, with 2025 likely to bring forth more "near term market uncertainty around the timing of an inflection in the home improvement market, especially for larger ticket discretionary spend."

The Consensus Forward Estimates

For now, LOW has already offered a mixed FY2025 guidance, with revenues of $84B at the midpoint (+0.3% YoY), comparable sales growth of +0.5% YoY, operating margins of 12.35% (-0.15 points YoY), and adj EPS of $12.275 (+0.3% YoY).

Given the inflated net debt levels at -$33.35B (+3.8% YoY/ -111% from FY2019 levels), the bloated inventory levels at $17.4B (+3% YoY/ +32.1% from FY2019 levels), and the impacted inventory turnover ratio at 3.2x (compared to a year ago at 3.4x and FY2019 levels of 3.73x), it is apparent that the company's turnaround process is likely to be prolonged.

Despite the impacted sales, LOW aims to "ramp up new store builds" with 2025 potentially bringing forth "five to 10 new stores," underscoring why its FY2025 Free Cash Flow generation is likely to disappoint, attributed to the higher capex by +30.2% YoY.

These reasons may be why the consensus forward estimates appear to be mixed in FY2025, with any meaningful top/ bottom-line recovery only likely from FY2026 onwards, as similarly observed in the prolonged normalization in the inflation rate through 2027.

Even so, we believe that LOW remains well capitalized to cover $2.6B in annualized dividend obligations, on top of the ongoing share retirement with -2% of its float retired over the LTM and -26.9% since FY2019, as the consensus continue to project robust cash flow generation ahead.

LOW Valuations

Despite the headwinds, LOW continues to trade at stable FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 18.78x, compared to the 5Y mean of 17.87x and 10Y mean of 17.75x, aided by the moderation from the 1Y mean of 20.11x.

At the same time, LOW is still trading attractively relative to its peer, Home Depot (HD) at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 24.35x.

The same conclusion is also observed in LOW's cheaper FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 4.32x against HD at 6.03x, despite the notable premium against their 5Y means of 3.88x/ 3.44x and 10Y means of 1.10x/ 1.69x, respectively, attributed to their intermediate term impacted adj EPS growth profile during the challenging year ahead.

So, Is LOW Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

LOW 5Y Stock Price

For now, LOW has lost much of its H1'24 gains, as the stock appears to bounce at the support levels of $220s while retesting the next resistance levels of $230s ranges.

Even then, given the ongoing market volatility, it is uncertain if the recent recovery may last, since the company's prospects may be impacted upon the intensification in reciprocal tariffs by the new US administration, worsened by the latest "proposal to impose a 20% tariff on most imports."

If anything, readers must note that LOW "source, both directly and indirectly, a portion of the products we sell from foreign manufacturers, with China and Mexico being the dominant import sources" for "our private branded and national brand merchandise."

These headwinds are likely to impact its profit margins assuming that the management chooses to absorb the additional costs, or sales, assuming that the additional costs are passed on to consumers at a time of tightened discretionary spending.

This is especially since the tariffs are "not explicitly included or incorporated in our guide in terms of what’s recently been enacted," with it potentially triggering a performance miss against the management's FY2025 guidance.

At the same time, readers may want to note that LOW is trading at a premium compared to our fair value estimates of $217.80, based on the management's FY2025 adj EPS guidance of $12.275 and the 10Y P/E mean of 17.75x.

Based on the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $15.01, there appears to be a minimal margin of safety to our long-term price target of $266.40 as well.

As a result of the near-term uncertainties and minimal upside potential, we prefer to prudently rate the LOW stock as a Hold (Neutral) here.
